title: Google and Industry Partners Announce Agentic Resource Discovery Specification for AI Agents
gist: Google and ten industry partners (Microsoft, GitHub, Hugging Face, Cisco, Databricks, GoDaddy, NVIDIA, Salesforce, ServiceNow, Snowflake) launched the Agentic Resource Discovery (ARD) specification, an open standard letting AI agents publish, discover, and verify tools/APIs/services across organizational boundaries via domain-published catalogs and federated registries, complementing MCP's invocation layer with early real-world adoption already in GitHub Copilot's Agent Finder and Hugging Face's Discover Tool.

themes.theme: A new discovery layer, complementary to MCP

insights.claim: The Agentic Resource Discovery (ARD) specification addresses an earlier stage of the agent-tooling lifecycle than the Model Context Protocol (MCP): where MCP defines how an agent invokes a tool, ARD defines how agents discover those tools in the first place, and is designed as a complementary discovery layer that works across frameworks and providers rather than replacing existing standards.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: Clarifies where ARD sits in the emerging agent-infrastructure stack relative to MCP, relevant for any team building or evaluating agent tooling infrastructure investments across the discovery-vs-invocation layers.
insights.quote: The problem is simple to state and hard to solve, especially in the enterprise, where the answer can't just be 'find something that works.' It has to be governed, with security and identity built in rather than bolted on.
insights.locator: Srinivas Krishnan, distinguished engineer at Google Cloud

insights.claim: ARD introduces two core constructs: per-domain machine-readable ai-catalog.json files describing available capabilities (tools, APIs, skills, agent endpoints), and registries that aggregate catalogs so agents can search by task intent instead of relying on hardcoded integrations or static endpoint lists, while remaining compatible with execution standards like MCP and OpenAPI.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: Defines the concrete technical mechanism (catalogs + registries) organizations would need to implement to make their tools/APIs discoverable to external AI agents.
insights.locator: The specification introduces two core constructs

insights.claim: ARD builds in domain-based ownership and verification mechanisms so agents can validate the authenticity of a discovered resource before connecting to it, explicitly intended to reduce risk in environments where autonomous agents may trigger actions across third-party services and enterprise systems.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: Addresses a real security gap in agentic ecosystems -- unverified tool discovery -- that could otherwise enable spoofed or malicious tool endpoints to be invoked by autonomous agents.
insights.locator: Trust and verification are central to the design

insights.claim: ARD was developed with contributions from ten major companies -- Microsoft, GitHub, Hugging Face, Cisco, Databricks, GoDaddy, NVIDIA, Salesforce, ServiceNow, and Snowflake -- and early real-world implementations are already live, including GitHub's Agent Finder in Copilot and Hugging Face's Discover Tool, both using ARD for runtime capability discovery at launch.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: Broad, near-simultaneous multi-vendor backing (including direct AI/cloud competitors) plus shipped implementations signals ARD is converging into a real industry-wide discovery standard rather than a proposal likely to stall.
insights.locator: The specification was developed with contributions from

insights.claim: Microsoft's principal AI engineer clarifies ARD's design goal is not a single global catalog of every resource but a federated model with many discovery services, each defined by what it indexes, whom it serves, and how it ranks results -- and explicitly states ARD does not replace authentication, authorization, governance, or organizational trust decisions.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: Sets realistic expectations that ARD is additive discovery infrastructure only -- organizations still need separate authorization/governance layers, meaning ARD adoption doesn't eliminate demand for enterprise AI-governance tooling.
insights.quote: The goal isn't a single global catalog of every resource. There will be many discovery services, each defined by what it indexes, whom it serves, and how it ranks... ARD helps AI clients discover capabilities, but it doesn't replace authentication, authorization, governance, or organizational trust decisions.
insights.locator: Jennifer Marsman, principal engineer in AI at Microsoft
